serving.natural_language_portion: A typical culinary serving of dried oregano is approximately 1 teaspoon (about 1.5 g), used as a seasoning in sauces, marinades, pizza, and grilled dishes.

nutrient_source.title: Spices, oregano, dried — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID: 170172)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for dried oregano used as primary nutrient reference. HPB Singapore values for energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, iron, and cholesterol are consistent with USDA SR Legacy and are retained. Saturated fat sourced from USDA SR Legacy (1.41 g/100g). Monounsaturated fat (0.71 g/100g), polyunsaturated fat (1.37 g/100g), and trans fat (0.0 g/100g) sourced from USDA SR Legacy. Water content (9.93 g/100g) from USDA SR Legacy. Vitamin C (2.3 mg/100g), vitamin A (85 mcg RAE/100g), thiamine (0.177 mg), riboflavin (0.528 mg), niacin (4.640 mg), vitamin B6 (1.044 mg), folate (237 mcg DFE/100g), phosphorus (148 mg), magnesium (270 mg), zinc (2.69 mg) from USDA SR Legacy.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of herbs and spices — analogous estimate based on low-carbohydrate dried herb category; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for dried oregano exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Dried oregano has an extremely high dietary fibre content (42.5 g/100g) and is consumed in culinary doses of 0.5–3 g per serving. GI is estimated at 15 (low) based on the analogous category of dried herbs with high fibre and negligible net carbohydrate contribution per serving. Glycemic load per 1.5 g serving = (15 × (68.92 − 42.5) / 100) × 1.5 / 100 ≈ 0.06, rounded to 0.1. At culinary doses, glycaemic contribution is clinically negligible.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (25 mg/100g) and very high potassium (1260 mg/100g) and calcium (1597 mg/100g); at culinary doses of 0.5–3 g, sodium contribution is under 1 mg per serving.
hypertension.guidance: Dried oregano is an excellent salt substitute for hypertensive patients; its negligible sodium and high potassium content support vasodilation, and using it liberally as a flavour enhancer can reduce reliance on added salt.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Exceptionally high dietary fibre (42.5 g/100g), low sugar (4.09 g/100g), and bioactive polyphenols (rosmarinic acid, carvacrol) with evidence-based hypoglycaemic properties; glycaemic load per culinary serving is negligible (<0.1).
type2_diabetes.guidance: Dried oregano is safe and beneficial for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; its polyphenol content may support insulin sensitivity and its high fibre slows glucose absorption, though clinical benefit at culinary doses (0.5–3 g) is as a functional spice rather than a primary therapeutic agent.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (1.41 g/100g), zero trans fat, zero cholesterol, and high polyphenol content (rosmarinic acid, carvacrol, thymol) with antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties; high potassium and calcium support cardiovascular function.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Dried oregano poses no cardiovascular risk at culinary doses and may confer cardioprotective benefit through its antioxidant polyphenols and potassium content; it is recommended as a flavour-enhancing herb to reduce dietary sodium in cardiovascular disease management.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Rich in anti-inflammatory polyphenols including rosmarinic acid, carvacrol, and thymol with documented COX-2 inhibitory properties; low saturated fat and absent trans fat.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Dried oregano is suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis and may be used freely as a culinary spice; high-dose oregano supplements should be avoided as they may interact with anticoagulant medications.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dried oregano (Origanum vulgare) is a minimally processed culinary herb produced by drying the leaves of the oregano plant, a perennial herb native to the Mediterranean region including Turkey, Greece, and Italy. The dried leaves appear as small green-grey flakes with a dry, crisp texture and an intensely pungent, aromatic, slightly bitter flavour profile derived from its essential oils, primarily carvacrol and thymol. Dried oregano is a staple seasoning in Mediterranean, Italian, Greek, Middle Eastern, and Latin American cuisines, used extensively in pizza sauces, pasta dishes, marinades, grilled meats, roasted vegetables, and salad dressings. Nutritionally, dried oregano is remarkable for its exceptionally high dietary fibre content (42.5 g/100g), very high calcium (1597 mg/100g), very high iron (36.8 mg/100g), and very high potassium (1260 mg/100g) per 100 g; however, culinary serving sizes of 0.5–3 g mean these values are not clinically significant in isolation. Its sodium content is very low at 25 mg/100g, making it an ideal salt substitute for hypertensive patients. The herb contains bioactive polyphenols including rosmarinic acid, carvacrol, and thymol with documented antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and COX-2 inhibitory properties relevant to cardiovascular disease, Type 2 Diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis management. Glycaemic load per culinary serving is negligible. Dried oregano is vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher.

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced from HPB Singapore (confidence 0.95) and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170172), which is an exact match for dried oregano. Secondary nutrients (saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, vitamins, minerals) sourced directly from USDA SR Legacy. All values are internally consistent and plausible for dried oregano.
notes: Cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero per HPB Singapore and USDA FoodData Central — dried oregano is a plant-derived herb and naturally contains no cholesterol. Trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for dried oregano. Vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0: naturally zero as dried oregano is a plant food with no B12. Vitamin_d_mcg set to -1: not reported in USDA SR Legacy for dried oregano. Vitamin_e_mg set to -1: not reported in USDA SR Legacy for dried oregano. Selenium_mcg set to -1: not reported in USDA SR Legacy for dried oregano. GI is an analogous estimate (15, low) as no direct GI measurement for dried oregano exists in the University of Sydney GI Database; at culinary doses of 0.5–3 g, glycaemic contribution is clinically negligible. The dietary_tag 'wholegrain' from the input has been removed as dried oregano is a herb, not a grain product. Calcium value of 1597 mg/100g is exceptionally high and consistent with published literature for dried oregano; this is a known characteristic of the herb but is not clinically significant at culinary serving sizes.
